Learning Python From a JS Angle (23): Classes — and Why JavaScript Isn't Quite OOP
Python
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle
13th iThome Ironman Contest

A quick, low-key chat about Python's class syntax. Along the way we untangle a question that bugged me for ages: is JavaScript actually object-oriented? Spoiler — kind of, but not the way you think. Then we build a Python class step by step, meet __init__ and self, and see how close it all feels to JS.

Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (22): Hitting the GitHub API
第 13 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

We've picked up a fair bit of Python by now, so it's about time we actually built something with it — that's how the concepts really stick. In this one we'll wire up the GitHub API with the requests package, let the user type in any GitHub username, pull back the repos that account has starred, and dump the result into a JSON file.
/en/python/2021/09/22/python-from-js-21-requests/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (21) - Requests
iThome Ironman Contest

In JavaScript, AJAX is one of those fundamentals you can't really skip — especially now that front-end/back-end separation is everywhere. So can Python do AJAX too? In this post we'll talk about Python's Requests library, and if you've ever used axios, you're going to feel right at home.

Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (20) - CSV

Last time we talked a bit about JSON. This time I want to add a quick chapter on handling CSV files. You'll run into CSV all the time when you do bulk imports, so it's worth pulling out into its own post. Here's how reading and writing CSV works in Python.
/en/python/2021/09/20/learn-python-from-javascript-19-json/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (19) - JSON
2021 iThome Ironman Contest

Now that we've covered basic file handling, the natural next step is reading files in different formats. This time we'll look at JSON — the format us front-end folks reach for all the time — and see why Python won't let you just dump a dict into a file and call it JSON.
/en/python/2021/09/19/js-to-python-18-file-handling/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (18): File Handling

This time we're going to talk about doing some file handling in Python — things like creating a file and writing data into a specific file. We'll cover open(), the different modes (r, w, a), reading and writing, and at the end the "plus" modes like r+ that let you read and write at the same time.
/en/python/2021/09/18/learn-python-from-js-17-version-management-virtualenv/
Learn Python From a JS Angle (17): Version Management and Virtual Environments
Learn Python From a JS Angle
iThome Ironman Contest 13th

Last time we covered the basic pip commands, but I left a couple of problems hanging without explaining how to fix them. So this post is all about the fix: using venv to isolate your project's packages (think package.json), and using pyenv to switch Python versions the same way nvm switches Node.js versions.

Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (16) - pip

We've covered a lot of Python's basic syntax in the earlier chapters, so now I want to take a detour and talk about pip. It's something you'll reach for constantly in real-world development. If you already know npm, pip is going to feel pretty familiar — here's a rundown of the commands you'll actually use.
/en/python/2021/09/16/learn-python-from-javascript-15-modules/
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (15): Modules
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle

Let's talk about modules in Python. JavaScript has its own idea of modules too, so we'll compare the two side by side — how to split your code into a separate file, export it, and import it back. Plus a couple of handy built-ins, dir() and help(), that make exploring modules way easier.
/en/python/2021/09/15/learning-python-from-javascript-14-bmi-calculator-2/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (14) - Building a BMI Calculator, Part 2

We've wrapped up a chunk of the fundamentals, so now it's time to put those little knowledge points to work. In this one we take the BMI calculator from earlier and add some real features — terminal input, a fake five-second Loading animation, and colored output text in the terminal.
